finding: §4.1 designates WorkflowOverride as the ONE canonical task-file workflow representation, yet §3.1/§3.3 require tp init --commit-strategy to write a commit_strategy field into that same workflow block ('--commit-strategy likewise'). But commit_strategy is not part of the v0.24.0 override/resolution set: it never appears in the §1 precedence list or §3.4's enumeration (quality gate, clean-round counts, timeout, caps), and WorkflowOverride has no place for it. An implementer switching task-file storage to WorkflowOverride cannot know whether to (a) add a new presence-tracked commit_strategy field to WorkflowOverride and, if so, how it resolves (it is in no precedence layer), or (b) store it outside the workflow block, which contradicts §3.3. As written the migration either drops commit_strategy or forces the implementer to invent an unspecified field and resolution rule.
suggestion: Either add commit_strategy to WorkflowOverride explicitly (as a presence-tracked *string) and state its resolution layer in §1/§3.4, or state that commit_strategy is out of scope / stored elsewhere and correct §3.1/§3.3's claim that --commit-strategy writes into the workflow block.

finding: §4.1 designates WorkflowOverride as the single canonical presence-tracked task-file representation and removes the defaults-applied Workflow struct from the storage path, but WorkflowOverride has no commit_strategy field (only the Workflow struct does). Yet §3.1 and §3.3 require `tp init --commit-strategy` to write commit_strategy into the sparse block, and §3.2 requires every present workflow field to survive round-trips unchanged. Making WorkflowOverride the sole storage type therefore silently drops commit_strategy on the first read-modify-write, contradicting §3.2/§3.3.
suggestion: Either add a presence-tracked commit_strategy field to the canonical override (and state it in §4.1), or drop the --commit-strategy claims from §3.1/§3.3. Explicitly enumerate every field the canonical override must represent (quality_gate, commit_strategy, the five convergence/timeout ints, checks) so none is lost when the defaults-applied Workflow leaves the storage path.

finding: §4.2 requires that 'no command reads an effective workflow value off a defaults-applied struct' — but the spec never enumerates which commands currently do, and today TaskFile.Workflow is a defaults-applied value type, so any command that reads tf.Workflow.<field> directly today receives the built-in default. Once §4.1 removes default-application on unmarshal, every residual direct read silently returns a zero/absent value instead of the default (e.g. an empty quality-gate command or a 0-second gate timeout). This makes the change a hidden multi-site bundle, not the localized 'correctness fix to serialization and tp init' that §2 item 4 (Minimal surface) implies. Missing even one read site is a silent regression the acceptance tests (§10) would not catch.
suggestion: Enumerate (or require an audit of) every effective-value read site that must be rerouted through ResolveWorkflow/EffectiveWorkflowForTaskFile, and add a mechanism that makes a raw effective read fail to compile (e.g. an unexported storage field or a distinct type) so no direct read survives. Reword §2 item 4 to acknowledge the read-path surface.

finding: "The `checks` array is written only when non-empty or explicitly set; an unset `checks` is absent, not `[]`." §4.1 mandates omitempty optional/pointer fields, under which an empty slice is omitted regardless of intent. So an explicitly-set empty `checks: []` (meaning: override the project config's checks to none) serializes identically to an unset `checks` (absent → inherits the project's checks). "written when explicitly set" and the omitempty mechanism contradict for the empty-array case: two engineers write opposite assertions for a `checks: []` override (stays `[]` present vs. becomes absent), and there is no way to represent an intentional empty override.
suggestion: State explicitly whether an intentional empty `checks` override is representable. If yes, specify its serialized form (keep `checks: []` present, not omitempty). If no, say so and note that projects cannot override project-config checks to empty; pin the expected JSON for both the unset and the explicit-empty cases.

finding: §4.3 makes a specific serialization claim for the `checks` array (unset → absent, inherits the project config via replace semantics), but the Tests/Acceptance section (§10.1–§10.5) contains no test that exercises `checks` at all. The most subtle field in the presence-preservation model has no acceptance criterion.
suggestion: Add an acceptance test for `checks`: assert an unset `checks` is absent and inherits the project config, and pin behavior for an explicitly-set `checks` (resolving the §4.3 ambiguity).

finding: The round-trip acceptance test exercises only `tp add`, `tp claim`, `tp done`, `tp set` (non-workflow), and `tp reopen` — but the §3.2 invariant also lists `tp close` and `tp remove`, and `tp commit` writes the file too. The acceptance criteria do not cover the full invariant, so a passing suite can leave `tp close`/`tp remove`/`tp commit` round-trip behavior unverified.
suggestion: Extend §10.2 to cover every command named in §3.2 (at least add `tp close`, `tp remove`, `tp commit`), or reconcile the two lists so the acceptance matrix matches the invariant exactly.

finding: §3.1 states the biconditional invariant that a workflow field is present 'if and only if it was explicitly set'. But §1 defines the bug precisely as fields present yet 'the project never set' (default-materialized), and §7.1 keeps such legacy task files unthinned, reinterpreting their present-but-never-set fields as explicit overrides. The present-implies-set half of §3.1 is therefore false for legacy (pre-v0.26.0) files, and the tool still cannot distinguish auto-materialized presence from a genuine override — the exact condition §1 describes. Stated absolutely, §3.1 contradicts §1/§7.1.
suggestion: Scope §3.1 to task files created or written under v0.26.0, and note explicitly that legacy materialized files are grandfathered: their present-but-unset fields are indistinguishable from overrides and stay so until deliberately thinned (§7.1/§9.4).

finding: §3.1 states the block contains a field "if and only if it was explicitly set" and "a never-set field is absent." §7.1 says an existing materialized file's default-valued fields — which no one explicitly set, they were written by old `tp init` — "read as explicit overrides" and are never thinned. So for imported/existing files the iff of §3.1 does not hold: a never-explicitly-set field is present. A tester reading §3.1 asserts imported default fields are absent; reading §7.1 asserts they persist — contradictory tests.
suggestion: Scope §3.1 to what tp writes ("tp never writes a field it was not told to set") and state that presence in a pre-existing/imported file is always treated as an override regardless of value, making §3.1 and §7.1 consistent.

finding: Presence-preservation is defined at the field level inside `workflow`, and §3.2 pins that `"workflow": {}` stays `{}`. But the case where the top-level `workflow` key is entirely absent from a source file (an authored/imported task file with no `workflow` object at all) is unspecified: does writing it back preserve the missing key, or normalize it to `{}`? Two engineers write contradictory assertions.
suggestion: Specify round-trip behavior for a missing top-level `workflow` key — either preserve absence or normalize to `{}` — and add a case to §10.2.

finding: The list of task-file-writing commands that must preserve round-trip ends with an open-ended "…" (`tp done`, `tp close`, `tp set`, `tp reopen`, `tp remove`, `tp add`, `tp claim`, …). The command set under test is not closed, so two engineers build different test matrices — e.g. is `tp commit` (which records `commit_sha`) in scope? `tp import`? `tp use`? "any task-file-writing command" is asserted but never enumerated.
suggestion: Replace the "…" with the exhaustive list of commands that write the task file, or define the class precisely (e.g. "every command that acquires the write flock") and enumerate its current members so the test matrix is deterministic.

finding: §3.2 lists `tp set` among the task-file-writing commands whose round-trip 'leaves the set of present workflow fields unchanged', but §6.1 says `tp set --workflow <field>=<value>` adds or updates that field — it changes the set of present workflow fields by design. The unqualified `tp set` in §3.2 contradicts §6.1; only a non-workflow `tp set` is round-trip-neutral, as the §10.2 parenthetical 'tp set (a non-workflow field)' implies.
suggestion: Scope §3.2's invariant to commands and `tp set` invocations that do not intentionally mutate the workflow block (e.g. 'excluding the single field a `--workflow` set intentionally adds/updates'), so it no longer contradicts §6.1.

finding: §3.2's round-trip requirement lists writing commands with a trailing ellipsis ('tp done, tp close, tp set, tp reopen, tp remove, tp add, tp claim, …'), leaving the complete set of task-file writers for the implementer to discover. Two writers that materially affect the workflow block are omitted and untested in §10: tp import (auto-wraps bare arrays and auto-fills coverage, constructing a TaskFile that can re-materialize the workflow block) and tp commit (records commit_sha on a task, a full read-modify-write). Presence-preservation for these paths is neither asserted nor verified.
suggestion: Replace the ellipsis with the canonical, complete list of task-file-writing commands, explicitly including tp import and tp commit, and add a round-trip acceptance case for tp import in §10.

finding: `--quality-gate` is pinned to an exact serialized field (`{"quality_gate": "<cmd>"}`), but `--commit-strategy` is specified only as "likewise" with no JSON field name given. A tester cannot write the assertion for `tp init --commit-strategy <v>` without knowing the field key (`commit_strategy`? `commitStrategy`?) or its valid value set. §10.1 tests only `quality_gate`, so the gap is unguarded.
suggestion: Pin the exact serialized form for `--commit-strategy` (e.g. `{"commit_strategy": "<value>"}`), enumerate its valid values, and add it to the §10.1 acceptance test alongside `quality_gate`.

finding: §3.3/§10.1 require tp init to write the literal 'workflow': {} and §3.2 requires that empty object to survive round-trip, but the spec pins presence only for fields WITHIN the workflow block (§3.1), never for the workflow object itself at the TaskFile level. It is therefore undefined what a round-trip does to a task file that arrives with NO workflow key at all (a minimal authored/imported file): does the writer add 'workflow': {} (adding structure, arguably violating §2 item 2 'must not add a workflow field') or preserve the key's absence? The implementer must guess whether the workflow key is unconditionally emitted or omitempty-omitted when empty.
suggestion: State explicitly whether the workflow key is always emitted (non-pointer / no omitempty, always '{}') or preserved-as-absent when the input lacks it, and reconcile that choice with §2 item 2's 'must not add a workflow field' for the no-key input case.

finding: §3.5 states invalid explicit values (e.g. review_clean_rounds: 0, outside 1-10) 'remain a validation error, not a silent fallback', but neither §6.1 (tp set --workflow) nor §3.5 specifies WHEN and HOW that error surfaces: does tp set --workflow review_clean_rounds=0 reject at write time (exit code 1, a hint, and nothing persisted) or does it persist the invalid override into the sparse block and only fail later on every subsequent read? For a presence-preserving block the latter poisons the file. The error path (exit code, hint text, write-aborted vs write-persisted) is left for the implementer to invent.
suggestion: Specify that tp set --workflow validates the value before persisting and, on an out-of-range value, exits 1 with an actionable hint and writes nothing to the task file — and add an acceptance case asserting the invalid set is rejected without mutating the block.

finding: "the review loop requires 3 clean rounds" names no observable — no command, output, or exit code demonstrates the requirement. Two engineers verify it differently (counting loop iterations vs. `tp review --status --check` exit behavior).
suggestion: Pin the observable, e.g. "`tp review <spec> --status --check` exits non-zero until 3 clean rounds are recorded, then exits 0."

finding: "an invalid explicit `review_clean_rounds: 0` is a validation error" does not pin the observable exit code or the command that surfaces it. A deterministic test needs the exit status (tp's convention is 1 for validation) and the triggering command (`tp validate`? the failing write?).
suggestion: State the expected exit code (1) and the command that emits the error, so the assertion is deterministic.

finding: tp init (no workflow flags) is mandated to write an explicit "workflow": {} empty object, and round-trips must preserve it as {} (§3.2, §5.1, §10.1, §10.2). An empty object carries zero override information: the resolver already treats an absent workflow key identically to {} (fall-through to project config / built-in default). This is a stored container emitted for structural determinism, not to feed any agent decision, and it contradicts the spec's own minimalism — §4.3 correctly makes an unset checks absent (not []), and §2 principle #2 declares 'absence is data' — yet the whole block is forced present-but-empty. The strictly cheaper and more principled representation of 'no overrides' is to omit the workflow key entirely.
suggestion: Make the all-unset case omit the workflow key rather than writing {} (matching §4.3's treatment of unset checks and §3.1's field-level absence rule). If {} is deliberately retained for determinism, state that rationale in-spec so the asymmetry with §4.3 reads as an intentional constant-cost choice rather than an oversight.

finding: §3.4 claims `tp config --resolved` "attributes each field to the layer that actually supplied it," but only the `source` label strings "task" and "project" appear (here and in §10.3). The exact label strings for the built-in, env, and CLI layers are not pinned in this spec, so a test of built-in attribution (`{}` block, no project config → source: ?) has no expected value.
suggestion: Enumerate the exact `source` label for every layer (e.g. "cli", "env", "task", "project", "builtin"), or reference the v0.24.0 definition explicitly, so attribution tests are deterministic.

finding: §4.2 says effective values 'come only from the resolution path (ResolveWorkflow / EffectiveWorkflowForTaskFile)', but that path itself returns a defaults-applied Workflow value (built-in defaults are materialized at resolve time). The next sentence — 'No command reads an effective workflow value off a defaults-applied struct' — then literally contradicts the preceding one, since the sanctioned resolution output IS a defaults-applied struct. The distinction the section wants is storage-struct-with-defaults vs resolution-struct-with-defaults, which the wording collapses.
suggestion: Reword to distinguish the two Workflow instances: forbid reading effective values off the task-file storage struct (defaults injected on unmarshal), while permitting the resolution path's freshly resolved Workflow — e.g. 'no command reads effective values off a task-file struct that baked in defaults at unmarshal time'.

finding: "its values equal the project config, so thinning changes no effective value" is asserted as fact but no check verifies that the materialized values in `spec/0.25.0.tasks.json` equal `.tp/config.json` before thinning. If they differ, thinning silently changes effective resolution, and no acceptance test guards this migration step.
suggestion: Add a pre-thinning assertion (or documented manual check) that each materialized field resolves to the same value after thinning, and treat any mismatch as a migration blocker.

finding: §8.1 asserts spec/0.25.0.tasks.json's materialized workflow values 'equal the project config, so thinning changes no effective value' and thins the block to {} on that basis, but gives no verification step. If any materialized field's value differs from what .tp/config.json plus the built-in defaults resolve to, thinning to {} silently changes an effective value — a behavior regression in tp's own repo — with nothing in the migration to catch it.
suggestion: Require the migration to confirm resolved-config equivalence before and after thinning (e.g. compare tp config --resolved output pre/post) and only thin fields proven equal to the resolved value.
